html:
Thulecite is a craftable Item found in the Ancient Pseudoscience Filter. It requires 6 Thulecite Fragments and a Broken Ancient Pseudoscience Station to be crafted. Thulecite cannot be prototyped and Characters must stand near an Ancient Pseudoscience Station to craft it.

Its main use is as a crafting material for items in the Ancient Pseudoscience Filter. Additionally, it can be used to repair a Broken Ancient Pseudoscience Station, opening up more crafting options.

Thulecite can also be acquired by destroying Ancient Statues with a Pickaxe, Gunpowder etc., or gathered from Cave Holes with the The Lazy Forager. After killing the Ancient Fuelweaver, an event triggers that respawns the Ancient Statues in the Ruins, providing a renewable source of Thulecite and Gems. Players must wait 20 days until the Ancient Fuelweaver can be fought again.

Thulecite has a 10% chance to be found in Ornate Chests and a 75% chance in Large Ornate Chests. It can also randomly drop when destroying Relics or damaging Ancient Pseudoscience Stations with a Hammer or by other methods.

With the Forgotten Knowledge update, Thulecite can be gathered by utilizing the Dust Moths found in the Ancient Archive. After feeding one an Amberosia, it will begin to ornate a nearby Tidy Hidey-Hole with mineable Thulecite Fragments. Because Amberosia is renewable, this provides another reliable (albeit slow) source of Thulecite as an alternative to killing the Ancient Fuelweaver. Additionally, with the Ancient Echos update, Thulecite Fragments are a possible drop from Boulderboughs, providing another renewable source.

Tips

  • Although Green Gems are somewhat rare, it is possible to use a Construction Amulet to craft 5 Thulecite Suits for 15 Thulecite, then use a Deconstruction Staff on those 5 Thulecite Suits to get 30 Thulecite back for a profit of 13 Thulecite (the Amulet costs 2 Thulecite). This practice makes it possible to collect big amounts of Thulecite from a few Green Gems.
  • Slurtles, Snurtles and Rock Lobsters will attempt to eat dropped Thulecite, so players should be careful and collect all Thulecite before they successfully eat it. Slurtles will also attack a player holding Thulecite if there aren't any other minerals around for them to eat.
    • When a Slurtle or Snurtle has eaten 2 pieces of Thulecite they will produce Slurtle Slime. When players feed Thulecite to a Rock Lobster, they will become friendly and follow the player around for 3 minutes (6 Clock segments).
  • If players need to turn Thulecite back into Thulecite Fragments, they can craft Thulecite Walls and hammer them. 1 Thulecite becomes 6 walls which yield 1 fragment each, making this process completely reversable at the cost of hammer wear.

Trivia

  • Thulecite may have been based off of real-life mineral thulite, which is also a variety of zoisite.
  • Like much of the Ruins, Thulecite was inspired by the Cthulhu Mythos, and may have derived its name from the name of Cthulhu, an ancient god-like being associated with insanity, magic, abnormal geometry, and tentacles.
    • A secondary inspiration for the name may have come from Thule, a northern region in ancient geography that is usually described as being the farthest north one can travel. Later, writers would use the term Ultima Thule to describe something far off and unattainable. This is more than likely inspiration for Thulecite's status as an endgame material.
  • The material itself is quite similar in color and use to the Dwarven Metal that is found in the The Elder Scrolls series, as both the Dwemer and the Ancient Civilization used their own material (Dwarven metal for the Dwemer, and Thulecite for the Ancients), and when they became extinct, their respective material were left behind and could be used by those who found their ruins. This is perhaps unsurprising, as it's a believable explanation for the rarity of the resources required for rare elite items.

“I wonder where this is from?”
–Wilson
“I don't think it would burn.”
–Willow
“Is pretty rock.”
–Wolfgang
“Its presence marks the edge of the unknown.”
–Wendy
“A MOST AESTHETICALLY PLEASING MINERAL”
–WX-78
“This seems to be an interesting, ectoplasmic residue-bearing variety of zoisite.”
–Wickerbottom
“It's the wood of the caves!”
–Woodie
“An exceedingly rare but useful material.”
–Maxwell
“This material pössesses gargantuan strength!”
–Wigfrid
“Fancy rocks!”
–Webber
“Thule-... thulec-... it rolls off the tongue, does it not?”
–Warly
“Rocks from old times”
–Wormwood
“I love working with new materials.”
–Winona
“I'll use the nearest shrine to produce a design.”
–Wortox
“Look weird, florp...”
–Wurt
“Rocks from an ancient civilization!”
–Walter
“If I could just break it down into more manageable pieces...”
–Wanda

Its main use is as a crafting material for items in the Ancient Pseudoscience Filter. Additionally, it can be used to repair a Broken Ancient Pseudoscience Station, opening up more crafting options.

After killing the Ancient Fuelweaver, an event triggers that respawns the Ancient Statues in the Ruins, providing a renewable source of Thulecite and Gems. Players must wait 20 days until the Ancient Fuelweaver can be fought again.
Thulecite has a 10% chance to be found in Ornate Chests and a 75% chance in Large Ornate Chests. It can also randomly drop when destroying Relics or damaging Ancient Pseudoscience Stations with a Hammer or by other methods.
With the Forgotten Knowledge update, Thulecite can be gathered by utilizing the Dust Moths found in the Ancient Archive. Because Amberosia is renewable, this provides another reliable (albeit slow) source of Thulecite as an alternative to killing the Ancient Fuelweaver. Tips[edit | edit source]
Although Green Gems are somewhat rare, it is possible to use a Construction Amulet to craft 5 Thulecite Suits for 15 Thulecite, then use a Deconstruction Staff on those 5 Thulecite Suits to get 30 Thulecite back for a profit of 13 Thulecite (the Amulet costs 2 Thulecite). If players need to turn Thulecite back into Thulecite Fragments, they can craft Thulecite Walls and hammer them. 1 Thulecite becomes 6 walls which yield 1 fragment each, making this process completely reversable at the cost of hammer wear.
Trivia[edit | edit source]
Thulecite may have been based off of real-life mineral thulite, which is also a variety of zoisite.
Like much of the Ruins, Thulecite was inspired by the Cthulhu Mythos, and may have derived its name from the name of Cthulhu, an ancient god-like being associated with insanity, magic, abnormal geometry, and tentacles.
A secondary inspiration for the name may have come from Thule, a northern region in ancient geography that is usually described as being the farthest north one can travel. Later, writers would use the term Ultima Thule to describe something far off and unattainable. This is more than likely inspiration for Thulecite's status as an endgame material.
The material itself is quite similar in color and use to the Dwarven Metal that is found in the The Elder Scrolls series, as both the Dwemer and the Ancient Civilization used their own material (Dwarven metal for the Dwemer, and Thulecite for the Ancients), and when they became extinct, their respective material were left behind and could be used by those who found their ruins. This is perhaps unsurprising, as it's a believable explanation for the rarity of the resources required for rare elite items.

summaryViHtml:
Tóm tắt

Thulecite là một Vật phẩm có thể chế tạo được tìm thấy trong Bộ Lọc Giả Khoa Cổ Đại. Nó cần 6 mảnh Thulecite và một Trạm giả khoa học cổ đại bị hỏng để chế tạo. Thulecite không thể tạo nguyên mẫu và Nhân vật phải đứng gần Trạm Giả khoa học Cổ đại để chế tạo nó.

Tips == * Although [[Green Gem]]s are somewhat rare, it is possible to use a [[Construction Amulet]] to craft 5 [[Thulecite Suit]]s for 15 Thulecite, then use a [[Deconstruction Staff]] on those 5 Thulecite Suits to get 30 Thulecite back for a profit of 13 Thulecite (the Amulet costs 2 Thulecite). This practice makes it possible to collect big amounts of Thulecite from a few Green Gems. * [[Slurtles]], [[Snurtles]] and [[Rock Lobsters]] will attempt to eat dropped Thulecite, so players should be careful and collect all Thulecite before they successfully eat it. Slurtles will also attack a player holding Thulecite if there aren't any other minerals around for them to eat. ** When a Slurtle or Snurtle has eaten 2 pieces of Thulecite they will produce [[Slurtle Slime]]. When players feed Thulecite to a Rock Lobster, they will become friendly and follow the player around for 3 minutes (6 [[Clock]] segments). * If players need to turn Thulecite back into Thulecite Fragments, they can craft [[Thulecite Wall]]s and hammer them. 1 Thulecite becomes 6 walls which yield 1 fragment each, making this process completely reversable at the cost of hammer wear. == {{Pic32|Placeholder}} Trivia == * Thulecite may have been based off of real-life mineral [[Wikipedia:Thulite|thulite]], which is also a variety of [[Wikipedia:Zoisite|zoisite]]. * Like much of the Ruins, Thulecite was inspired by the [[Wikipedia:Cthulhu_Mythos|Cthulhu Mythos]], and may have derived its name from the name of Cthulhu, an ancient god-like being associated with insanity, magic, abnormal geometry, and tentacles. ** A secondary inspiration for the name may have come from [[Wikipedia:Thule|Thule]], a northern region in ancient geography that is usually described as being the farthest north one can travel. Later, writers would use the term Ultima Thule to describe something far off and unattainable. This is more than likely inspiration for Thulecite's status as an endgame material. * The material itself is quite similar in color and use to the [https://en.uesp.net/wiki/Skyrim:Dwarven Dwarven Metal] that is found in the ''[[Wikipedia:The_Elder_Scrolls|The Elder Scrolls]]'' series, as both the Dwemer and the Ancient Civilization used their own material (Dwarven metal for the Dwemer, and Thulecite for the Ancients), and when they became extinct, their respective material were left behind and could be used by those who found their ruins. This is perhaps unsurprising, as it's a believable explanation for the rarity of the resources required for rare elite items.
